title: Text Generation Before Transformers — N-gram Language Models

questions.question: What does an n-gram language model estimate?
options: P(next word | previous n-1 words) from count statistics
questions.explanation: An n-gram LM models P(w | last n-1 words) via counted occurrences.

questions.question: What problem does smoothing solve in n-gram models?
options: Zero-probability assignment to n-grams unseen in training, which collapses sentence likelihoods to zero
questions.explanation: Smoothing reallocates probability mass so unseen n-grams get non-zero probability.

questions.question: What insight makes Kneser-Ney smoothing better than naive absolute discounting?
options: It estimates the lower-order distribution with continuation probability (number of distinct contexts a word appears in) instead of raw frequency
questions.explanation: Continuation probability gives credit for context diversity, not just raw count.

questions.question: What does perplexity measure?
options: exp of the average negative log-likelihood per token on a held-out test set; lower is better
questions.explanation: Perplexity = exp(- mean log P); lower means the model is less surprised by the test text.

questions.question: Why must train and test sets use identical tokenization when comparing perplexity numbers?
options: Perplexity depends on the tokenization scheme; mismatched tokenizers produce noncomparable scores
questions.explanation: Different tokenizations change the token count and likelihood, making perplexity values incomparable.

questions.question: Why do generated trigram-LM sentences feel locally fluent but globally incoherent?
options: Local trigram context guides each next word but the model has no long-range memory beyond n-1 tokens
questions.explanation: Conditioning only on the last n-1 tokens makes long-range coherence accidental.

questions.question: Where do n-gram models still ship in production in 2026?
options: Latency-critical paths like speech recognition rescoring and on-device autocomplete via libraries such as KenLM
questions.explanation: KenLM-style n-gram models still serve as fast on-device or rescoring components.

questions.question: Why is computing an n-gram baseline before declaring a neural LM 'good' still recommended?
options: If a transformer LM does not beat a tuned Kneser-Ney baseline by a wide margin on the same tokenization, something is off in the training pipeline
questions.explanation: KN baselines are surprisingly strong; a neural LM should win by a large margin or you have a bug.
